title: Glyph Desk
desc: Expired credentials pile up quickly when the whole hive moves through one gate.

steps.text:
Not every problem at Queen's Gate wears armor. Some arrive as chipped clay, smeared seals, and workers insisting that a nearly unreadable ID glyph should count because everyone involved is very busy. The renewal desk sits just off the main arch where expired passes are replaced, damaged ones are repressed, and gate clerks try to turn irritation into paperwork before it clogs a whole lane.

The renewal clerk slides two trays toward you. "Blank glyphs need today's seal and lane code," she says. "Returned glyphs need sorting by damage and expiration. Pick the cleaner task if you like. Neither one avoids complaints."

options.label: Stamp the blank glyphs with fresh seals and codes
Sort the returned glyphs by type of failure

steps.text:
The blank glyphs are pressed clay discs stacked in felt-lined trays, one side smooth for the day's seal, the other waiting for the lane code and watch mark. You stamp them in sequence, checking each impression for clarity before setting it on the drying rack. Too shallow and the recognition stone hesitates. Too soft and the edge deforms in the first pocket it meets. The desk handles too many passes for either mistake to be treated lightly.

Once the rack is full, the clerk inspects the line with a speed that suggests she has done this longer than most creatures have had credentials. She finds no weak impressions, which seems to disappoint her mildly. "Good," she says. "That means the gate can blame the carriers instead of the desk for once."

options.label: Set the rack beside the desk and clear the stamp table

steps.text:
Returned glyphs fail for different reasons, and the gate likes those reasons categorized. Some are expired but intact. Some are chipped at the edge and must be reminted. Some are so worn from handling that the lane code has become a suggestion. You sort them into neat groups while the clerk fields a steady stream of explanations that all amount to the same thing: someone did not renew when they should have.

It is tedious work until the value of it becomes obvious. Once the failures are separated, the line moves faster. Intact expired passes go straight to renewal. Damaged ones go to remint. The hopeless ones stop wasting everyone's time. The clerk glances at the cleaned-up trays and allows herself a brief look of relief. "There. Three piles, three answers. Order is underrated until it fails."

options.label: Stack the sorted trays and leave the desk moving smoothly
